

[Shawn] All right, let’s see everyone, I’m gonna drop the question in chat as best as I can. And then I’ll read it quickly. So this is from Carma about module eight. This may have been covered already, so please excuse it if it has. “If you have people going through your value newsletter soap opera series, maybe even a story power promotion, but at the same time, you’re also running a product launch series. Do you care that the person is getting the SPP and the PLS at the same time? Will this not cause some confusion? How can we do this efficiently? There’s a obviously technical answer here that André will be far better equipped to answer. And then there’s a functional question, which will both probably say the exact same thing, but no, of course not, we don’t wanna have people getting 12 different things at the same time. If we’re doing a PLS, they should get the PLS. If they’re in the story powered promotion, they shouldn’t be able to get the PLS. So you just, I don’t know, André, how do you handle this?

[André] Yeah, I mean, so the technical side of it is inside of module 11. It’s all there. I’ll show you how to do it, depending on which way, or depending on how you want to do it. So for us, we don’t do anything different. We don’t prevent anybody from seeing one thing and not the other, but at the same time, as whenever we’re doing a PLS, we’re not sending newsletters. Very rarely do those two things overlap. But we don’t do anything to prevent somebody from receiving a newsletter. Should we be in the middle of a PLS at the same time? Although I can’t remember offhand when that’s happened. I think there may have been a time when a PLS has ended on a Friday, which is the same day that we send a newsletter and so they have just received two emails on that day, which is fine because, only a small segment of people are ever in a PLS. The rest of the world are not in it. So, it’s just those people that you’re communicating with in the PLS and they’re okay with it ’cause you’re communicating with them and they know when I use it, it goes out. So they received the newsletter. Everybody else’s only receiving the newsletter. But I think there’s other nuances. So if you have a certain soap opera series that may be, Flaka Julie’s 126 pages long. So when somebody is working their way through that, you may want to prevent them from getting certain things, so in that case, you can see in module 11, how we stop them from receiving pending newsletter, for example.

Here’s the background, it say’s, “Hi guys, I’m loving AOE so much depend through ARM, but this is the first time I’m applying the knowledge a lot more intentionally, no questions on how to implement, especially since this isn’t a cookie cutter program, I’m adapting what you said based on my context, my big questions for now are around mindset and mastery, especially when it comes to doing a product launch, which they’re doing this month.” For those of you who don’t know Ian, Ian is, I believe his business partner is COO with Michael Simmons, Michael does mental models club and the Seminole Writing Program. Okay, so questions around mindset. I can’t help but marvel at your conversion rates 10% plus. It’s inspiring and makes me want to double down on applying your teachings. I know that will take time to get there. At the same time, it’s discouraging in the short term. For our most recent PLS, we sold to a list of 900 customers. And our conversion rate was a 1.66%, a far cry from 10%. And then he goes through some more information, their list size, that she’s already mentioned 900 conversion, 1.66% opt out rate, 0.2% complaint rate, zero, and then sort of the email rate, the email open rate, click rate, nothing really out of the ordinary. We did a beta test launch at the same time back in February, but we changed the audience targeting and corresponding substantially for this launch. Most of the sales will come after 90 days. That is sometimes after the launch, we should sell it again. We’re making plans to do so in 2022 in Q1. Any other mindset advice you have for me so that I continue to support our customers, whether they purchased or not? What a great question. Okay, I don’t even know where we start with this. So yeah, I mean, there’s a lot to unpack here. And the first rule of systems theory is that you have to define the boundaries of the system. And wherever you define the boundaries of the system, is going to like that has enormous consequences. So for example, if we build a system where we have a Facebook ad to the landing page, to an email series, to an offer, and we put the boundaries of the system around the, and we want to evaluate the performance of the Facebook ad, and we just put the boundaries of the system for that analysis around the ad itself. Like how many people click on it? What’s the interaction rate? What kind of social signals do we get through commenting, likes, et cetera? Like that changes everything. We can optimize the hell out of the ad and make no improvement to the system at all. And we can actually make the system much worse. And this happens all the time, not to go off on a tangent about paid traffic, but when you focus myopically on an ad, for example, and you optimize the hell out of it, or make it maybe you’re a little more broadly focused and you focus on the ad and opt-in rate for lead gen to the big, so you can drive those numbers through the roof and actually make your overall conversion rates worse. So the first thing we have to do with Ian is draw the boundaries of the system where they’re most valuable, and that can’t be at just the list size and conversion rate. Because if we don’t know what went into getting that list, then we have no idea what could have affected that conversion rate. So let’s say for the sake of argument, let’s look at two different alternatives. And I don’t know which of these is true. I assume neither of these is true to be clear. I don’t know anything about how that list was generated, but let’s assume that option one is we were to say, “Hey, there’s this amazing thing happening. And we’re gonna give away all these great ideas. And by the end of the next week, you’re going to have 10 new ideas about how to generate content, click here to be on that list.” Well, of course, if you do that, you’re going to have a lot of people who are interested in what you just described, the value proposition you just described. They’re not interested in a very expensive offer, which this offer Ian is talking about is, compare that to you at the other end of the spectrum. If in generating that list of customers, you were to say, here’s what we’re doing. Here’s what it’s all about. The focus is on this product and this product is not right. This course is not right for most people because, A, it’s a year long program, B, it’s very expensive. Like you go through the whole list. So people who are now it’s eyes wide open. So now that list knows what they’re getting into before they add themselves to the list. So I don’t know how this list of 900 customers was generated. But without knowing how it was generated, there’s no way to know whether or not that conversion rate is extraordinary, if it’s good, bad, or otherwise. And it just, you can’t possibly, con you can’t compare that rate to our 10% plus rates without knowing how we generated the interest list and what we said, what was the value proposition versus what Ian and Michael did. It just, you have to know how that list was created. So the sort of answering, I don’t even know if there’s really, so mindset advice. Yeah, mindset advice number one is define the boundaries of the system in a way that’s meaningful for you, that it’s going to give you the answers that you want. So it can’t be the boundaries of the system to evaluate an email campaign, cannot be at the edge of the email campaign. You have to know what generated the list first, and really understand, like think through from a perspective of a prospect, what were you? This is the same reason we don’t like lead magnets. when you offer somebody a lead magnet, it changes everything that follows. It can’t help it, right? And it doesn’t, it’s not a question of, is a lead magnet good or bad? There is no good or bad. They’re just consequences, that’s it. You make a decision, there’s a consequence, period. And the consequence doesn’t have to be negative, it could be positive, but, I think I’ve said this maybe on every single call that we’ve had. So their mindset, as soon as they get on, it’s just thinking, even if it’s the same person, it’s just thinking differently as opposed to, like we mentioned just now, instead of getting this thing, this lead magnet, the carrot, it’s different, There’s tension, that’s pulled them on and that tension exists as soon as they’re on the list for the first time. And they want to be a part of this world, as opposed to just releasing the tension by downloading the PDF with the 11 hacks on how to do X, Y, Z, and then that tension is gone. And so as everything else downstream of that detention. So, there’s many ways to do this badly perhaps, and doing it a well just takes time that relationship. But there’s something fundamentally different about email and maybe I’m weird. Well, obviously I’m weird, but maybe I’m weird in this particular way, but if I get an email with a video link in it, I very rarely click on it. I don’t know why that is. And maybe it just needs more setup, but I don’t video delivered by email. I don’t know, again, maybe strange, but that just doesn’t to me, that’s jarring. It’s not as easy to engage with. And I watch, I consume plenty of video. And so I could, I mean, I could see like an interesting video series as a Facebook, series of Facebook ads, retargeting video by video. That would be kind of interesting, but then you get, weird consumption stuff. I could see that being okay. But there’s one question that Denise asked that I think is important to draw attention to, which is, where did she say it? I can’t remember how she asked it, but it reminded me of something that I know in my agency days dealt with a lot, which was you send traffic to a landing page or landing page could be opt-in or it could be a front end product. And let’s just say for the sake of argument, that that landing page has a VSL. Now some percentage of people, bail and they barely look at anything, right? So we use qualitative factors like bounce rate and other things to figure out who we target. But let’s assume somebody went to a VSL. I hate VSLs. So I’m particularly sensitive to this, especially VSLs that don’t have controls, pisses me off all day long. And VSLs where I can’t control the pace. Like my little video controller doesn’t let me speed it up. That drives me bat-shit crazy too. So I hate, hate, hate VSLs. So if I were to go and see a VSL or see some intro copy and see a VSL, I might be interested in the offer, but I’ve gone. And I don’t think it’s just me. I think there are plenty of people. So I would also do in the past, this is not rocket science by the way, is if depending on what the front end was. If the front end was video, the initial one, and people who stayed longer on that page and say 10 seconds, but didn’t become leads or whatever it was, I might re-target them with a text version of the same content. Also, conversely, do the same thing with video. If we targeted people and there was text on the landing page and do the exact same retargeting with video, because there’s some percentage of people respond to one or the other better. It’s a second bite at the apple. Does it make the difference between success and failure? No, but it does confirm this idea that some people respond more to video than they respond to texts. Some people respond to text more than video, but I think again, you have to understand the context. And the context of email, is that your in general, I think expecting to read words on a page, read words on a screen. So in that sense, I don’t know. I mean, you can certainly apply all of the principles of serialized storytelling for video. I just, I wouldn’t, I just wouldn’t try to do it in email. It feels like too much of a juxtaposition for me. And there are other ways to do video better than to try to embed them in emails. I guess I could. [André] I haven’t had any gum yet. So thinking out loud, how I would probably do this is, I would create a mix of media. So the idea behind everything would be to create a very powerful journey for somebody to go through and through the experience of going through that journey that would use different mediums to where they would interact with different mediums based on which medium is gonna create the most impact for somebody. And it’ll almost always be a mix in this case. So it would be, I’m gonna say long form, but not necessarily long form long form. So it’s a webpage where there’s texts. So someone’s reading. And then there could be inline video where it makes sense to have a video component. That’s doing something that’s different to the text so there’s a reason why it’s suddenly changed, could have beautiful inline images where it helps explain a point that can’t be done very easily, what the written word or in video. So there’s a special component and it’s this interaction between text. So the written word and watching something and seeing images to get an idea through. And it’s obviously serialized. So there’s many pages linked together in this little journey. And you use email too frame and draw people to this place because you can’t create that experience in email, many people are reading emails on their mobile phones. You can’t have beautiful inline images in there. They’re small and tiny and you can’t even see the detail, videos don’t play in line. Or at least I think we’re getting to a point where you can play somebody as in line, but I mean, in an email depending on the client, but it’s just awkward, but having an amazing email experience inside of an email. So there’s just some texts, maybe a few hundred words, that’s it, that set up the frame and it creates the tension that pulls somebody to the webpage and then the webpage does what it does well, where it can display beautiful media and create an experience. And you could use audio narration for the text part. So, but I don’t think the audio narration would narrate the entire block of texts, especially when you’re using video that strategic, like the video needs to be there because the video is doing something special. So the blocks of text, you could have say there’s a, I don’t know, let’s just say this 500 words of text. Well, there could be an audio button that just reads that 500. But then it stops and it says press play on the video. So if somebody is just listening, they understand that they’ve got to do something and interact with the page. The point is for them to interact with this thing. So they’ll go and watch the video. And there would be an image there if need be, and then they would continue to read again or take the payback and then kind of listen to the next written component. And it would move them through the sequence of pages that obviously go somewhere there’s obviously some call to action. Something that happens that is, where it relieves the attention, whether it’s something to bar or something else to do. But I think that’s how I would do it. And then obviously that content on the website, it is part of your world-building efforts. It’ll get indexed by Google, Google loves indexing videos as well. There’ll be many opportunities for search engines to send you people that would then see this page called and interact with it and end up on your email list and the cycle continues. So I think that’s what I’ll do. And that’s my answer.

[Shawn] And I might say, let’s make sure that we also draw attention to the fact that, David Foster Wallace’s idea of the fishes and aware of the water, you’re asking two writers what we think about using video. So right? That’s I mean, that’s the water that we’re all suspended in here. So if you add one of our good friends is a world-class video marketer. If you asked Greg the same question, he’s gonna give an entirely different answer and give you a very compelling reason why, sequencing videos is far more powerful than texts because of X, Y. So again, there’s no right or wrong. It’s just consequences, just downstream effects. And I think one of the things that gets missed in this question a lot is when somebody says yes to whatever it is that brings them inside the tent in your world, what happens after that? Because if you sell all texts and then you deliver all video, that’s a mismatch, right? If you sell all video, deliver all texts, you get a weird mismatch. And it just be aware that one thing does affect the other. Now, if you mix and match and how you deliver, maybe you do some live calls, maybe you do some audio, maybe you do some video and some written thing, then, okay, mix and match on the front end. But you just have, again, it’s where the boundaries of the system we’re talking about. And if it’s lead gen, some sort of intensification by email, phone call or some sort of consultative call, or I guess there’s a masterclass consultation call some sort of point of conversion course, like we’ve got to look at that whole thing to really understand how to answer some of these questions. And don’t ask writers about video. That’s the other message here too. Or take our opinions that we grade with a very hefty grain of salt. There’s a follow-up that he’s asked here too, which I’ll run through. So it says, “People enter my world by signing up for the free masterclass. That’s how they start receiving my emails. Until now I’ve been sending the same emails to the people who attend the masterclass and into those who do not attend it. Would you recommend two different RBS sequences?” And then the next question is , “What are your strategies to unsubscribe people, and referring about the people that don’t open the emails. I’m guessing that the ones do open stay until they decide it’s not for them.” Okay, so. Yes, the first place would you recommend to different RBS sequences? Well, I don’t know. I mean, this is an interesting question. So it says you’re sending the same emails to the people who attend the masterclass and those who do not. So did they attend, they need to go back and figure out where does the masterclass happened by email, where let’s see. Denise, why don’t you let us know in the chat. Is the masterclass, do people sign up for the masterclass and then get that delivered by email first? It’s a webinar, okay, all right. So, all right, I got you now. So here’s the bottle. Somebody signs up for a webinar and then they either attend or they don’t, but what happens next is the same. All right would you recommend too. Yes, and maybe they’re well, there, again, it becomes a question of just-in-time like somebody can watch the webinar immediately, or is it live once a week, twice a week? That changes the dynamics of things, because in general, for a webinar, you want to have some kind of attendance intensification, right? So you want to do something and there’s experts at this out there. Joel Erway is one of them. I’m trying to think of others but you can find some great free resources on this too, but you want to have, if you’re doing something by webinar, your very first priority is to get people on the webinar. And it’s very easy to sign up for a webinar, much more difficult to actually put your ass in a chair and attend a webinar. So and just in time is great for a variety of reasons where someone can just choose to watch it now, scheduled is great because you can intensify, by email beforehand and let them know what you’re getting the value that they’re going to get. One trick that I can’t remember where you did this. I thought it was brilliant, which is you create a webinar study guide, and it’s only like two or three pages, but it’s the whole thing. It’s a PDF, but it’s a marketing document. And it’ll say things like, and it of course follows the webinar in sequence that it’ll say something like, the very most important thing about whatever your topic is, is dot, dot, dot. And then there are like three lines where you can write it in. So of course when you look at, it’s a huge open loop. Like, “Whoa, wait a minute, I’m going to learn like the most important thing about X, Y, Z.” And then three ways to expand your, whatever the thing is about, to maximize your profit. Then you’ve got 1, 2, 3 with lines, like they’ve got to write it in. So of course when I’m looking at this document, I’m like, “Oh wow! I’m gonna learn all of these things because you’re showing me the questions, but I have to go to the webinar to get the answers, right?” That’s that’s a very, I don’t want to say basic, it’s a very time-honored time-tested strategy to get people onto a webinar. So to get back to Denise’s question, “Is it two different sequences?” Yes, it has to be, but it’s three sequences. Unless it’s just in time and again, I get hung up on just in time because you have to get people on the webinar. And then after the webinar, that’s the traffic cop, right? Someone who has attended the webinar has now, has an entirely different set of ideas and information in their head that you can build on over the next X number of emails that leads to the next step, the person who has not attended the webinar doesn’t have that. And you can’t take them to the next step. They’re not a good prospect yet. So if someone signs up to attend a webinar and they don’t attend, then pick a number three, four, five, and you could sort of look at this two ways. You could just build a relationship with them, like take a step back like, “Well, they didn’t attend the webinar. So I’m gonna stop talking about the webinar. I’m just gonna talk about,” you’re going to create an RBS that, establishes that empathy and authority. And then later email four or five, six, whatever it is, mentions the webinar again, see, this used to build a relationship and then you sort of return back to the webinar itself and say because they’ve already they’ve said no, with their behavior, right? So you have to take that into consideration. People vote with their behavior. So when someone says, “Hey, I want to do this webinar, but I’m not actually gonna do it.” First of all, they’ve told you, they’re probably not a great prospect. But they may have just also told you, “You know what, life got in the way.” So take a step back and realize someone raised their hand. They took a step towards you, but they didn’t go the full way towards you. So how do you have a conversation with that? Right? And that conversation is different than someone who raises his or her hand and says, “Yeah, I’m interested in this thing. And they actually put their ass in a chair and they go watch the thing. Well, now you have a different conversation. You’re much, if it’s an hour long webinar, you’re an hour ahead in your relationship with them. So how do you build on the relationship now? We can’t assume there’s no way in hell. It’s the same conversation with those two people that they’re not the same people at all. I think in an ideal world, it’s this isn’t true, than a ton of stuff. I like webinars. I think webinars can be amazing. So I, what I was going to say is, I might do relationship building first webinar after, and then whoever attends the webinar, gets like a soap opera series or something that intensifies the phone call, and whoever doesn’t gets relationship building series to value newsletter with periodic references to the webinar. I think you can do that same thing and just remove the initial relationship building series, send them straight to a webinar because you’re actually building a relationship in the webinar. So that’s another way to think about it too. That’s doing the heavy lifting. Well, André take it over and say something smart. So I was wandering all over the place and he says just in time. So, we’re not doing the front end relationship building.

[André] Yeah, I mean, this is everything that we do is about building relationships, right? So any relationship that’s not quite there yet, that’s certainly new. The dynamics around getting people to do things is gonna be very different than the same person after they’ve been in your world for a year, two years, or five years. So I don’t know, but and again, webinars, I’m not jumping onto any webinar live, that’s just me. If I was a customer of of email, I wouldn’t be on this call now I would be waiting for the replay, because then I’ll–

[Shawn] Don’t tell everybody that!

[André] And I’m a good customer, I’ll buy stuff all the time. So I’m just saying that there’s different types of people that value their own time differently to other people. So for me, because I’ve got a lot of stuff going on and there’s windows of opportunity for me to pay attention to something. And also I like to stack my attention around certain things. So, ’cause I’m terrible at multitasking across different things about different ideas, different, whatever. I find it tremendously difficult. So I stack my thing. So when I’m writing fiction, for example, I need to get into that mode and it takes a little while, and then I’m in a turn and I’ve got to stay in it for as long as possible. And I can’t con do something else. So when it comes to webinars on any topic, it could be fiction or nonfiction or anything, but, I’m joining them with the hope that I get a repay link. And if I don’t get a replay link, it’s done. I’m not gonna email people and ask them for replay links. But, and I just won’t, I’ll just miss it so.

[Shawn] You’d like to Denise’s webinar, that was just in time every hour. So you can do whatever the hell you want. So you’re perfect. Whatever she’s selling, you’re perfect for it. He just said, Denise, he buys stuff. So send André a link to your webinar.

[André] But I mean, I think it’s an interesting, something just popped into my head. You have to think about what is a webinar if we put it in art of email terminology, right? If we just trying to figure out how it fits in and using that terminology, it really is a relationship building series, right? It’s doing the same thing because you’re taking somebody from who the hell is this person. And I don’t know, like mildly to largely distrustful, right? And you’re establishing the foundations of a relationship with that prospect. So in many ways, if we think about like, how does this fit into the art of email? It fits in with the same idea of like, somebody is signing up for an RBS, but specific to a course. So they’ve given implicit permission about whatever the topic of the webinar is being of interest to them. So this kind of sets up this opportunity for webinar to relationship building emails, to soft bridge, to something to get them on a consultative call. That lines up pretty well because a lot of the relationship building can happen in that webinar. So I think that model works. But again, I still, I think there’s a tremendous dividing line. You do not pass go just like you don’t pass go until you get a relationship building series. You don’t pass go until you watch the webinar. That’s the deal. Someone can’t and I’ve seen this happen a million times. I’ve had clients whose like, “Well, if they didn’t attend the webinar, can we still, how should we sell to them?” And I’m like, “You don’t!” And if people look at me like, I have not looked at them like I had nine heads, like, why wouldn’t you do that? ‘Cause it’s like, why do you want a customer who has already said no to like the lowest barrier thing that you’re going to have them do? Like what kind of customer is that going to be? Right, so, I mean to get, if you can imagine someone’s like, “Yeah, I can’t be bothered to get on this webinar on a 45 minutes to hell with that. I’m not gonna do this thing that’s gonna be beneficial to me.” Do you want that person on a consultative call? Hell no. I don’t want to try to sell that person. So I think the webinar’s the gate that all good prospects have to pass through and if they don’t do it immediately, then the relationship building series, it’s whatever, seven emails, gets that really builds, it’s empathy, it’s credibility. You mention the webinar again at the end. I wouldn’t mention it every single day, but I would first three emails, empathy, authority, credibility, maybe email for you. Soft bridge mentioned again, the webinar value proposition of the webinar. The last three, let’s say it’s seven parts. First three are, or six parts. First three are relationship building series. Second three are continuing to build the relationship around what you’re talking about in the webinar and you send them back to them. And if they go to the webinar, then they kick off the post webinar series. If they don’t go to the webinar, they get your value newsletter over time, but they don’t see and they don’t get to do anything until they go to the webinar. You just nurture that relationship over time. That’s how I would do it.

[Shawn] Yeah, let me unpack some of that, how I’m hearing that, because if a webinar is considered the same as a relationship living series or similar, we don’t, somebody ops and they receive an RBS and they get to the end and then something else happens. So they’ll need to get added to the newsletter loop or they’ll get put in, they can raise their hand to get put into something else or, they go into a story pod promotion or something like that. But we don’t create like a firewall at the end of an RBS and then delete all the unopens. So, I’m just thinking out larger. So why would we do that with a webinar? So maybe there would be these two RBS’s and the one is, go listen to the downward whether or not, and whatever’s a good open rate. So with the emails anything, let’s say 50, 60, 70%, for somebody raising their hand for something, is the good thing. Well, you could tag those people. So when they get to that number inside of the webinar, it puts them onto the different RBS because they’ve obviously paid attention and they’ve interacted, they’ve listened. They’ve heard what they need to listen to. And then that obvious kicks in so different one. And anybody that hasn’t showed up or just, they never got past a go. They just get the normal hobbyists that puts them into the newsletter after that. Unless that’s what you said already, Shawn, I’m just trying to, you might have said something similar to that anyway.

[Shawn] Yeah, I mean, you’re jumping to the next part of the question, which is do you, what’s the strategy to unsubscribe people? Strategy isn’t really to unsubscribe people. Now, okay all right, so, and again, iOS 15 just changes all of this, right? So here’s the question as it’s asked is what’s your strategy to unsubscribe people. I’m referring to the people that don’t open the emails, well, we don’t know accurately anymore who doesn’t. Doesn’t open emails. Thanks apple. So, and this is the other thing too. This is something to be very careful with. And we all, the imperial, we all fall victim to this, which is sort of two things. One, we tend to think everybody buys like we buy or interacts with information like we interact with information. And we tend to think that, and our opinions about what we’re doing are clouded completely because we’re inside the bottle trying to read the label. So there are going to be people who, for whatever reason, they see an ad, they see whatever they see or whatever it is that sets up your masterclass webinar and they’re like, “Hell yes, I need this.” And they sign up. And then something goes sideways for them, right? The car breaks down, the kid gets sick. Whatever it is. But life gets in the way. Or they’ve got a million other things coming at them. They’re really interested in this. It’s on their radar. Some people, I may know somebody who does this, will do something. Will sign up for something or we’ll buy something. So it becomes a placeholder because they know they need to do that thing later. I’m referring to myself here in case that’s not clear. So don’t assume that, we hopefully want to believe is that the person is staring intently at their tablet screen, their desktops and their mobile device. They’ve signed up, they’re waiting with bated breath to hear the genius we’re about to lay down on them in our masterclass. And they better well do it right now. And then as soon as they do that, they better do. And that’s just not how life works. This is no different than assuming that you’re the window to make a sale closes really fast. Data is very clear. That’s not true. People buy when they’re ready to buy, not when you’re ready to sell. So the same thing is true in this context with a webinar, that you wanna have plenty of people who for all the right reasons, sign up, they expect to watch it at the top of the hour and then life gets in the way and they don’t. And then they get the first email from you and the second, and maybe they read one maybe they don’t, maybe they don’t read any of them that week that they get, let’s say they get seven over seven days and they don’t read a damn thing. And you think to yourself, “You know what, screw you! You’re out of here, buddy! Right? But that person again, maybe they never read those emails, but then your first newsletter comes in two weeks later and they’re like, “Oh, shit, right! I signed up for that thing before. I need to look at that.” But they don’t, life gets in the way again. Context, whatever it is. But then they get your next newsletter. And there’s a tidbit in that one. And you build a little goodwill with them. And then weeks go by. Maybe they don’t open another newsletter. But five months from now, that person finally says, “Oh my, I have to solve this problem that I originally identified five months ago when I signed up for that webinar.” But you’ve had five months when you’ve just been sharing value, establishing yourself as a preeminent authority in that space. And now they’re ready. And when they then think to themselves, “Oh, now I gotta deal with this now, right? Whatever it is, now, I got to finally sit down and deal with this.” Who were they going to go to? The brand new person who they stumble on in Facebook or are they going to go to the person who has been building a relationship with them kindly gently tirelessly over five months? Of course, they’re gonna go back to you. So don’t assume the data is overwhelming on this. Very small minority of people who are going to respond positively are going to do that in a short timeframe and just build the damn relationship and reap the benefits. You just don’t know when you’re going to get them, but you can trust that you’re going to get them. So there, that’s my thought. Okay, and well, let’s move on. Let me think. Now, this is a fun question. I guess we’re in the other, yeah, Denise was in the other question. These are the remaining questions. I’ll answer this remaining questions. Okay, my blood pressure’s going up. It’s after this question too. All right, deep breaths. I’m going to have you answer this question. Next question first. Deep breaths, deep breaths. Do some yoga while you’re doing this so I can calm myself. Okay, here’s Kim’s, this is the question I’m gonna lose my mind about to be clear. This is Kim’s question. “I’d love to hear more on how you think about creating your manifestos and where they fit into the art of email. Thank you.” All right, André. You’re the manifesto guy.

[André] Yeah, let me think about that. We don’t stick to, I mean, you’re not gonna go to school or university and learn how to do manifestos the way that we do it.

[Shawn] What!

[André] We do them our way. We’ve got some of our frameworks, on the site already. If you want a more formal way, I mean, and to be clear, your perfect one will be, we’ll probably be somewhere between how a more organic version and a very structured version. Jordan Peterson, he’s unpacked his entire process for writing an essay. So an essay isn’t necessarily, well, it’s not a manifesto, but there are elements that, some structural elements that there may be similar and he’s got the whole thing laid out at the essay on app. Might be worth looking at that, it’s thorough. I mean, it is everything you need to write a compelling argument in the form of an essay. and so that’s a good place to start. And then from that, maybe read some of our frameworks on the site. I think other than that, I think our idea of sharing manifestos is really partially us trying to externalize certain components of an argument that we can then just share with others. So it’s not a sales page. It’s just a sharing part of our philosophy that has connected very deeply with a certain product, for example. So with the sphere of influences that there’s this idea of prospects are made and the quality of a prospect matters. So it’s basically just our thinking in X many thousand words of while we believe that that is the way forward. So it’s not a sales page. It’s not doing the same lifting that a sales page does. It’s really just a sharing something of value in a way that’s kind of unpacks our arguments, but more thinking out loud, you know? So, and then we just put it into this thing that we call a manifesto. And that’s kind of, when we did the art of email one, we got to a point in the course where we couldn’t go any further, and externalizing certain stuff in the manifesto was important because it allowed us to then continue with the course because we had said stuff already that we know people would have read, but it’s stuff that we didn’t wanna put inside of the course, its place wasn’t to be inside of our course, it needed to be external to the course. Where could we put that? Well, that’s the manifesto because every single person that ended up inside of our email, really needed to have read the manifesto and most, I think many people did. But Shawn’s the structure guy, so I’m sure he’s got a very succinct answer for this.

[Shawn] Yeah, thanks, no pressure there. I appreciate that. Yeah, so first book recommendation, it’s a book called “Abraham Lincoln and the Structure of Reason.” Author’s name is David Hirsch. All right, so this was a weird experience for me. When I realized how Abraham Lincoln would sort of taught himself to think, I realized that’s how I think. Now to be clear, I’m not saying I think like Abraham Lincoln, it’s not what my point is, but the key part of that title is the structure of reason, right? So I’ve sat on I think every call and I say this all the time, I’m a very structural thinker. André is Anne Lamott on John McPhee when we talk about writing. That’s my conceptual standpoint. So when I think about a manifesto, the manifesto for me is very similar to the way that John McPhee talks about the lead. Like McPhee, can’t write, this is hidden in his words, he can’t write anything until he has a solid lead, I’m the same way. And where’s the lead? Well, if you were creating a course or if you’re selling or the lead often is in the manifesto, right? Like that’s where you’re setting or you’re making the case. And I tend to think like, and I’ve been called hyper rational and not, which I thought was a compliment, the person who called me that, and I realized that it was not meant as a compliment. So keep that in mind. But the way I think, I would say it’s hyper structural. So for me a manifesto, the way, and I’ve written a pre working with André, I’ve written the boatload of manifestos for clients. They sent a lot of traffic to manifestos and the way I’ve thought about it. This is not right or wrong. It’s just how I’ve thought about it, also unpack. And I hope I can unpack it. When I think about the manifesto then why it fits into the larger thing. So, the way I think about a manifesto and the way I’ve written a bunch of them, is I start like, sort of Stephen Covey’s idea. I begin with the end in mind. So I imagine that there’s this point at which right before opt-in, the person who has read the manifesto needs to have a certain X number of beliefs that coalesced into a single belief. And in one step, so from that single belief, then I have to essentially make my case like a lawyer. And I have to go back and say, Okay, if this overall thing is true, what would have to be true for that to be true? Well, this would have to be true and this.” And then I sequence them. And it’s a very step-by-step by step and that’s really what the book is about, it’s a structure of reason if you get, if someone says, “Okay, I understand that, gotcha. Yup, I understand that, okay, great. I understand this, great okay.” Well, if you understand those three things, here’s the logical conclusion of those three things. Like that’s how I think about, that’s the role the manifesto, now is that how it’s written? Not necessarily like there’s, the art of email manifesto has, we have characters who are introduced to, we introduced twice and we sort of use the characters as vehicles and we use, there’s sort of a structure to the manifesto that we rely on, like all of that’s true, but we’re making a case. And by the time you get to the end of that manifesto, we want to bring you to a point where the right people are nodding their head up and down, like, “Yup, this makes a lot of sense. Oh, that’s why it wasn’t working.” Or, “That’s why I’ve had this experience. Now I understand.” Like that’s the thing. And we’ve mentioned before, Amanda is on this call has an absolutely extraordinary, manifesto on the front end. So go read that so you can sort of see it in action. And I don’t know Amanda’s process. So it may be similar, maybe wildly different. I think they’re all, many roads lead to Rome in this example. But for me, it’s, I can’t, again, this gets back to the whole, like, what’s the lead. I can’t do this stuff after if I don’t know what set’s it up. And this is maybe just a blind spot in my thinking and my writing. And it’s a very big part of how I think and how I write. Like I can’t just start somewhere. I mean, I can from a thinking perspective, I do this all the time. Like, I’ll start with something, but then I have to put that something into a context, like, is this something foundational, right? Or is this something like derivative? And it’s 12 step to move because what I’m always trying to do, and this is just how I’m wired. It’s not right or wrong. It’s just how I’m wired. I’m always trying to find a foundation to stand on that feels irreducible to me. First principles, like what is the thing that we can stand on? And in the art of email, one of the primary thing that we stand on is this idea that, if you optimize for the short term, there’s a whole set of things that you can do that make perfect sense, until you realize that most of the selling that you’re going to do is gonna happen over a longer time period. Like if you accept this idea that you have this one opportunity to make a sale or a short window of time to make a sale, all of the decisions that we see being made in marketing make perfect sense. But the moment you realize that’s not true, then nothing that people do make sense. So for me, I’m always trying to like find out what is true here? And if that’s true, how do I build out from that so that the actions that I’m taking align with truth. This is sort of a very esoteric way to think about this, but it’s really this, it’s something that was expressed by Buckminster Fuller and Fuller said essentially, and I won’t get the quote exactly right. “Don’t fight forces, work with them, right?” So in the context of the art of email, we could fight this idea that people won’t buy in the short term, right? We could do everything and believe me, I’ve worked with plenty of clients who do. But at the end of the day, you can’t fight gravity, right? You can jump out of an airplane and say, “You know what, today I’m not a big fan of gravity.” And that’s going to be the last thought that you have as you rock it to the ground. Gravity doesn’t give a shit if you believe in it or not. It just is. The fact that most people don’t buy fast, doesn’t give a shit if you believe in it or not, it just is. So for me, like, I wanna know, like that’s where I wanna get to. And the manifesto is how I get there. And maybe it’s my own crazy that’s showing up, but for me, and the way that I’ve done it in the past, I think it’s my approach. But I find it very useful, I have a document that I just call compilation, whatever the manifesto is dash compilation. And I just put ideas in their. Quotes, ideas, thoughts, whatever it is. I just over time, let’s load it up. And then at some point I look at that and like, “Okay, there’s like 50 things in this document.” But those 50 things, like I start to put them together. Like this quote in this thought are really the same thing. This quote expresses this thought. Okay, so the 50 things aren’t 50 things. They’re probably 12 things. So I get down to a list of 12, I don’t know which 12 things like, okay, here are the 12 things. Like, what’s the big thing, right? Because what I want to do is I wanna sequence them. And I say like, “Okay, what’s the main point here?” If these 12 things are all points that I could share, first of all, are they reducible? Can I combine them, is there a larger idea here that I could put three or four of them into. So it’s simpler ’cause it’s hard to make 12 points, can somebody get them to remember it. We wanna try to be in the neighborhood three or so, three or five in that neighborhood, maybe seven. But I just I’m trying to get down to that level. So then I can sequence it into a case. Then I become an attorney and maybe all of this is because I wanted to go to law school, but I took the LSATs and I was not a chance in hell I was getting into law school, but I just tend to think like a lawyer. And I’m prosecuting my case with a manifesto because if I can get you to believe, because I believe it to be clear. I’ve made the case for myself, right? So if I can convince myself with an iron clad argument, with like well-structured proof to myself that this makes sense, then I damn well should be able to convince you too. And if I can’t convince myself, then I damn well shouldn’t try to convince you either. So if we couldn’t write that, the art of email manifesto, we had no business selling the course, plain and simple. Like if we couldn’t make that case to you and say, and not to go on a side rant here, but it’s two things with a manifesto. And this may be only important to us or me, I don’t know, André, may think I’m bat-shit crazy when I say this, but it is so important to me that what we say in the manifesto is never violated by what we teach in the art of email. There’s no daylight between the two. The art of email is the logical conclusion and a logical expression of the argument we’ve made in the case we’ve made in the manifesto without exception. And if we said something in the art of email that I could go back and look at the manifesto and go like, “Ooh, this isn’t true.” I couldn’t do it. Like my brain would just shut down because I have such a weird and André, there is this when we get into our occasional disagreements about how we’ve mutually viewed reality, a lot of it is because I can’t unclench my brain from something that feels like it’s not structured rationally. It’s so weird. Now, of course, I’ll end on this note, which is people don’t buy for rational reasons. People buy for emotional reasons. So that is a huge weak spot in my, if I were doing all the writing and only me, then this, it would not be nearly as good as it is. André is much better at putting the nuance and the emotion and all of that, that he does makes the parts that I do infinitely better. And people buy because of emotion. And that’s just fact, it’s just an old idea. It’s just over oversimplified, but generally directionally right that we buy for emotion, we justify with logic. But I’m not trying to sell with logic. What I’m really trying to do, is make a case so that I can feel really good about the selling. Like I can explain to you, this is the situation in which this is gonna be valuable for you. So you know how to make a good decision. And this is where this thing we’re talking about fits into the world. Like all of that to me is just a way, it’s a way to make my brain unclenched, and a way to be of service to our audience. And that’s the best way I can think about it. So it’s a very long answer to the question, “Where does the manifesto fit in?” I could not create a course without having the manifesto first. I just couldn’t do it because the manifesto starts the conversation. It sets everything up, and without the lead, I’m floundering like a turtle on its back. Done, okay.

[André] It’s also worth drawing attention to the fact that, manifestos and multi-page pre-sale sites, it’s just different expressions of a very similar thing is, we only want, any product, there’s an ideal customer for it. And part of putting people through these multi-page or a manifesto, is so we can draw out the people that are the best fit for the course, for the product. It’s not about sucking everybody and what can I say that’s gonna resonate with 80% of the entire population of a certain market. That’s not the point of it. It’s to weed people out. And we can do that through the lens of a manifesto or a multi-page presale site. Because we only want certain people that believe that a certain way forward is way forward. So, for art of email, it’s this idea that relationships is, everything starts and ends with that. And when you have a strong relationship, everything becomes easier. Every promotion becomes easier. Every news that you send out, more people are gonna read it and pay attention to it. And that’s all because of this idea of a relationship. So getting people into this course that don’t believe that, they just believe sell hard and fast, so the manifesto pushes those people away. So that’s it now, I’m done.

[Shawn] That’s fun. That was an interesting side, okay. So we’re gonna move on to Ian’s question. I’m going to read the question. I’m going to have you answer it while I work on a breathing exercise. And after you answer it, I’m going to try to answer it without absolutely losing my mind, which the likelihood of that happening, it’s so unlikely. Okay, here’s the question. So Ian had mentioned before that he was talking about mindset and mastery. So this is the mastery component of the question. And Ian and I are friends. So I’m gonna answer this with Ian. This is gonna be my answer to this, it’s just gonna be talking to a friend, so it’ll probably be a little more raw and it’s gonna be a rant. Here’s the question. This is more of a general question. “What’s your deliberate practice routine like? How do you become world-class at email copywriting? How many years would it take and you reckon. I’ve been putting in the time and effort to learn and practice. For one, I started to track email writing time, and also make sure I use a checklist each time I write, not looking for shortcuts, just your perspective on your own mastery journeys as fellow marketers and craftspeople.” All right, André, this one’s teed right up for you.

[André] Well, the first thing we do is is we get as many copywriting books as we can, and we would read and study them. And we put everything into a checklist–

[Shawn] I thought you’re gonna say we throw them out. We bought a copy of all the copywriting books, and then we put those in the trash. That’s not nice. Why don’t you say something like that? That’s terrible.

[André] Well, first of all, we are not email copywriters. So let’s get that first thing out of the way. We are just writers and we start by understanding and empathizing with the market, with the emphasis being on the relationship. At that point, writing is just a matter of reading a lot and writing a lot. And then just expressing that in the writing, there’s things you can do. Shawn, well, I mean, you can say what you do with Morning Pages, but we do Morning Pages and we do various practices like that, but that’s more, that’s email, writing emails, that’s a downstream effect of first showing up and just becoming a better thinker. And you can’t keep things in your head, so you’ve got to externalize them onto paper. And then you just interact with your thinking through a pen and pencil, and just get that clarity of thought about any idea, any concept, anything, and everything. And, Shawn does it every single morning. I do it, a version of that most days I tried to do it as much as possible. And then, yeah, just read a lot and write a lot. But, we certainly on our email copywriters and we don’t study copywriting books for the reasons Shawn expressed in a previous video. And I certainly don’t have a checklist. I don’t think Shawn does either. Maybe he does. Do you have a checklist?

[Shawn] You’re laughing at the idea that I never know, never know what I’m gonna write or say until I see it. So although, I mean, I think checklists could be a great tool. It’s all going to this book checklist manifesto is awesome. So then there’s two different types of checklists too. So I think two review, one of them is a two review, 2-Review, meaning the checklist is about things to review, which is good for like, if you’re gonna fly an aircraft. And it’s just, I think you could do actually, but that would be an interesting, I mean, we don’t do it, but again, it’s easier for us because everything’s easy for 20 years of experience. But I think certainly could have like a checklist. I wouldn’t write to a checklist, but I might use one to go back and just sort of ask myself like four or five questions. It wouldn’t be things like tactical stuff. It would be things like, have I written this from the perspective of the value for the audience first? Do I know the value I’m intended to, maybe I would phrase it this way. Do I know the value I’m communicating to my audience? Like, can you say it in a sentence? Like if someone said to you, “I don’t have time to read this email, what’s it all about?” In one sentence, can you tell them, “This email is about X, Y, Z.” so you can’t do that, you know what email’s about and that’s a great exercise to do when you can do it. If you’re an emergent writer like André, you might find out what it’s about after you write it. If you’re deliberate writer like I am, you can’t write it til you know what it’s about, which is but, that’s how I would use it, the checklist, right? But there’s one thought that I wanna mention before, well, two thoughts I wanna mentioned before I actually dive into the question itself and the hurt myself. So the first one is tactical, right? Good writing is good thinking. So spend more of your time learning how to think well, and your writing will get better as a result. Maybe you can go the other direction with this. I mean, maybe to learn to write better, you become a better thinker. I mean, Sonke Ahrens’ book, “Take Smarter Notes.” That’s a great place to go at it from the writing direction to be a better thinker. That’s such an incredible book. Just stupid, stupid, stupid, incredible book. Lots of books on how to be a better thinker. I mean, start with “Play-Doh” work your way up from there. Again, Plato Socrates Aristotle that’ll cover the big basis. Leslie, just asked about my books, “Take Smarter Notes.” I think Sonke Ahrens, S-O-N-K-E A-H-R-E-N-S. All right, so the other thought, which I think may be helpful for everyone is to understand that there’s a great, you’ve seen the movie “300” it’s about this Leonidas and the 300 Spartans who stopped the Persian advance, at the gates in Thermopylae, right? There’s a scene in there where the 300 Spartans meet some Athenian lawyers and the Athenians are mad because they feel like they’ve brought more soldiers and the leader of the Athenian groups say’s, “We expected to see a lot more soldiers. Why’d you bring so few soldiers?” So the Leonidas asks one of the Athenian soldiers, which are professional. He says, “I’m a potter.” And he says to somebody else, what’s your profession? “I’m a blacksmith.” And then he turns to the Spartans and says, “Spartans, what’s your profession, right?” And they’re all lawyers. So he says to the other general, “It looks like I brought more soldiers than you did, right?” So I love this idea of Spartans, “What’s your profession?” Right? For all of us to actually be able to answer that question in one sentence, because mostly because you’re gonna get answers that are so far away from what the reality is, like, “Oh, I’m a copywriter.” Yeah, yawn, “Oh, I’m an email copywriter,” bullshit, right? Like you’re gonna get whatever. So I’ll tell you what the answer is from my perspective. And I think it’s the answer for all of us, just to be clear with everybody. I think I’m in the enthusiasm business, right? And not fake enthusiasm. And I don’t have any interest in spending my life getting wound up. And trying to pretend like I’m excited about something and then trying to transfer that enthusiasm to others. That’s just too much work. So because I’m in the enthusiasm business, the very first thing I have to do, is I have to be enthusiastic about something. And the beauty of that is, I have full control over myself. I’m a practicing stoic. So I try not to try being the operative word in practicing, being the other operative word. I try to focus only on those things over which I have control. And I can control the things that I pursue, that the attention that I give those things, the depth that I’m willing to go to, to learn more about them, how and why I follow my curiosity. So at the end of the day, writing, thinking and writing, is an expression of enthusiasm. And sometimes it’s hard to write because I’m so excited about something. And here’s a perfect example. Tomorrow you’re gonna get a newsletter, and it’s a two-part newsletter that’s about creativity. And, I wrote the base email for tomorrow and I mentioned to André earlier, I emailed saying, I wrote it as kind of hysterical. It wrote itself while I was sitting there with my hands on the keyboard, right? Because this is just something that’s so important to both of us. That draft just emerged because it’s just there because it’s something we’re so excited about. And it’s leading to, we have a two part series. Each one has a hard bridge. Then there’s going to be the week of Thanksgiving in the United States. There’s gonna be a promotion for our ideas to ask the updated version of our ideas, assets and masterclass that we created two years ago. Well, I am so excited about this. And so enthusiastic about this topic that it’s like, when I sit down to think about writing, my hands are shaking, because just so much we wanna talk about. And we’re so excited about it. So if I can’t convey that enthusiasm through the written word, there’s no book in the world that’s gonna teach me how to do that, right? The writing is, that’s the mechanism, right? If I try it, but if I try to do it, like how do I get everybody excited about this thing that doesn’t excite me? What! Right, like when you are excited about it, like reading Julie’s SOS yesterday, it was very clear, she’s excited about this. It just comes through in the words, right? How does Julie need to learn to write better? No! She just needs to figure out how to convey her enthusiasm, which she was doing in 26 pages. Like, that’s it! That’s writing. It’s just a transfer of enthusiasm with a written word. And If you’re not, and so how do you learn to be a better writer? To get really curious, taking a lot of different information, let it percolate. And we’re talking about this over the next two, sort of a four-part process of creativity. And then when you sit down to write, just watch your fingers move on the keyboards and shit happens. It’s amazing. And I’m not belittling how hard writing can be. It’s how many ways at all, writing’s easy and you just sit down in front of a typewriter and bleed. Yes, writing can be hard, but when you’re really excited about something, it’s not that hard. If you getting better at the craft it can be hard and you might push yourselves, but it’s enjoyable hard. It’s a good kind of hard, okay? Now let me answer the question as it was written. So here’s the question again, “What’s your deliberate practice routine like?” Here’s the phrase in there that makes me wanna scratch my eyes out, which is deliberate practice. This is why I’m gonna go on a rant from all this. So if you’re not familiar with the term deliberate practice, it’s a very specific type of practice that was identified by someone named K. Anders Ericsson from University of Florida. Sadly passed away not long ago. K. Anders Ericsson is the person who Malcolm Gladwell grabbed the idea of 10,000 hours of deliberate practice and what I felt like if it was blank or whatever it was, popularize the idea of the 10,000 hour rule. So first of all, the 10,000 hour rule is bullshit. That’s not at all what K. Anders Ericsson said in his research. Malcolm Gladwell, who I used to love as a writer, and want to love as a writer, never had a single conversation with K. Anders Ericsson when he wrote the book. He never checked how he thought about it, where the guy who did all the research on it, which to me is unforgivable. It means I can’t trust anything that Malcolm Gladwell has written. And he’s such a wonderful writer, but I can’t read any of his books now. I can’t enjoy anything, like the ones that I read that I love, now I feel bad about. Because I wonder did he just kind of stayed through the book and just write what he felt like made sense? Like in my previous conversation for me, like needing to know that something is sort of true, it’s just such a betrayal. So, and the other thing about this quest is, that’s my sort of conceptual rant, but the other thing about deliberate practice, that’s really, really, really important to understand, it is a very particular type of practice. And nobody does it. If you’re not an elite athlete, you are not doing deliberate practice. When people are talking about deliberate practice, they don’t mean what K. Anders Ericsson was talking about. When you actually dig in and see what he was talking about. You’re talking about the 0.001% of the world, who are virtuosos at something. Who are in a very particular type of discipline that can be practiced a certain way. So, and Ericsson co-authored, wrote a book later called “Peak.” And this is where he distinguished what I believe he called Purposeful Practice. And purposeful practice is available to the rest of us, right? That’s when we take something. And I see this a lot in the martial arts, where instead of teaching somebody an entire movement, you might break that movement down into its component parts, and you teach the parts and you get lots of reps on the parts, which is focused practice, right? So, for writing and to my friend, Ian, I will tell you first and foremost, stop reading the deliberate practice research, stop reading all of that just because, it’s not what this is about. Writing is not a discipline that benefits from the model of deliberate practice, period. It does not. It does not fit the research. It does not fit how that framework was designed. And therefore it’s the fruit of poisoned tree. Everything that follows doesn’t line up. So think about writing. Writing is a lot more like jazz and improv than it is like playing scales. You can probably, and I think music was one of those disciplines that would line up well for deliberate practice. But you can probably play your scales and learn a song. And I think those are some of the examples that Ericsson used, using deliberate practice. But writing is jazz. Writing is improv, right? Writing is not playing scales. So it doesn’t benefit from that framework to think about. So of course, now that I’ve said how not to think about it, the question becomes, how do you think about it? In general, and I think André has written this in other places, people who write well say the key to writing well is to read a lot. I think that’s step one. You wanna be a better writer? Become a better reader. And better reader means lots of things. Go listen to Safi Bahcall’s interview with Tim Ferris where he talks about his different writing frameworks. And if you wanna talk about the ultimate open loop, but you still just makes me cringe when I say this out loud, and I’ve actually emailed Safi Bahcall and asked him if he’s ever closed this loop and he has and it’s making me crazy. But in the interview with Tim Ferriss, Safi Bahcall, I believe he said he has seven frameworks that he created for his writing, but he only gave four. And it drives me still to this day, and this was years ago, drives me bat-shit crazy I don’t know what the other three are. But one of them, and he uses acronyms, but one of them is listening, I can’t remember what he calls it, but it’s listening for the sound. It’s listening to the sound of the writing. And another is much more technical. That’s where I would start. Listening to how Safi Bahcall is a great writer. Has broken it down, and then read the way he’s describing in those frameworks. That’s a good first step. And you have to read a lot. There’s just no way around that. So, if you want to be a better writer and you have say a hundred units of time per week to do that, probably 70 or 80 of those units per week need to be in reading, maybe more. Especially especially for the first five, 10, 12, 15 years. André and I still read a tremendous amount. That’s the first key. It’s a more deliberate reading I think, like you might read something and then, like if I’m reading Jordan Peterson’s “12 Rules for Life,” he does the structure of how he makes his arguments, it’s really interesting to me. And I don’t really understand it yet. So, I’m reading the book and it’s one of those books that it’s so good you wanna get through it as fast as you can, yet you wanna savor it at the same time. So it’s a difficult read in that sense, but then you can also do the same thing with Michael Lewis’s book. ‘Cause I’m very interested in writing narrative non-fiction. So you can read Michael Lewis, and then read it, experience it, but they’d go back and say, “How the hell did he do this?” Right, like how did he create this chapter? Like, “Oh, he introduced this character.” How? How did he introduce the character? Why am I so connected to that character? Like dissect it. When you read something you like, dissect it and figure it out and start to make, like that’s the practice. But it’s not a practice where you break it down to this like, list of ongoing to do these three activities. Writing just doesn’t work that way. For whatever reason, writing is more emergent, I think. So I think that’s the answer, I don’t feel like it’s a great answer, but it’s, I guess a lot, Ian had mentioned the journey. Keep in mind and this is important for everybody, when you read what we wrote, keep in mind that André is widely recognized as the, if not the, I think most people would say the, if not the, certainly in the top three email writers in the world, right? I would certainly put them as number one. So when you read our stuff, that’s the first lens to think about. Second lens to think about is that, I was a professional journalist in DC for years before I opened an agency. I have probably written professionally for 27, 28 years after a graduate career of writing, I had to unlearn how to write as an academic where I wrote hundreds and hundreds of published pages as a graduate student. So I have 25, 27, 28 years of writing. It’s not 10,000 hours, it’s like 50,000 hours. So there’s that. So when you read our stuff you’re like, “Damn, how did they do that?” Well, it’s 45 years-ish of experience. So just recognize that. Don’t try to do that, try to do your thing that you did yesterday a little bit better today, right? Look back at something like, “Ah, I didn’t like how that felt.” All right, well, do it a little better today. And do a little better tomorrow and keep doing that. And then you’re going to wake up in five years, because five years are going to pass anyway. But you’re gonna wake up in five years and you’re just going to be infinitely better. I don’t think that writing benefits from that practice focused framework as much. That’s the end of my ramp. Yeah, Nick just mentioned that I’m doing “Project,” which is like the greatest, stupid, greatest book ever written. All right, your turn now.

[André] Yeah, I’ll just add that, if you’re gonna be doing the reading, read broadly read, I started off making the mistake. And I started reading very late in life when I was 35 years old. That was my starting point. So I’ve been playing catch up ever since, but when I first started, I just stuck to the genre that I liked, that was the most enjoyable. But then you only read that and therefore the style of read of writing is gonna be a constraint to that one thing. So even if you enjoy genre fiction, read widely, read literary fiction, it is like poetry done well. So even if the topic itself may not be great, or may not be your cup of tea, just read it. ‘Cause it’s just, it’ll improve your writing no end. There’s the book, I’ve just forgotten what it is now. Shawn waited a few months ago, I think, which is the one about trees,

[Shawn] Oh, “The Overstory.” Oh my God!

– “The Overstory.”

[Shawn] What a book! What a book!

[André] Something like that. It’s just such beautiful, beautiful writing. And I’ve just posted a link to a book that’s pretty good on how to read.

[Shawn] Calling what we do the same thing as what the author of “The Overstory” does, is a crime. I can say like like we all write, oh my God! Right, yeah, sorry. Anyway, yikes! Yeah, I just bought that book. Thanks by the way to help me. Yeah, Roy Peter Clark is incredible. But I think it’s a good point that maybe we can wind up on that, this question around buying books. Like there are so many books about writing, I mean, you can, if you really and here’s, we’ll use the thinking idea of inversion to answer Ian’s question. So if you’re not familiar with inversion, inversion is incredibly powerful tool, which is, instead of trying to answer, how did you X, you answer how did not X. And then you just avoid it, right? It’s like, so the question, the way that we could use inversion for Ian’s question is like, if you wanted to never get better at writing, what could you do? Like you just make a list of things that you could do. And then you just make sure you never go there and that’s Charlie Munger’s quote, I just, the only thing I wanna know is where I’m gonna die, so I never go there. So in inversion here, is a really powerful tool. Because if you really want to make sure you don’t improve your writing, try to find the best books on writing and buy them and then try to get through them. And like ’cause there are so many and you can spend so much time procrastinating, I mean, you could easily, easily, easily, spend the next three to five years learning how to write and not writing. I mean, just by buying the books, maybe go to masterclass and watch you a bunch of good writing. Like you could study all of that stuff for years and not get any better at writing. ‘Cause writing is an ass in chair discipline, and you don’t get better at it till your ass’s in the chair and the pens moving, or the fingers are on the keyboard. So if you really wanna figure out how not to get better, a good place to start is to buy all the books. I have most of them actually. But reading will get you there. Anything to add on this one before we move on?

[André] Yeah, it’s a funny Forward that I read the other day. So again, it’s a writing book, but it’s a very special one. So, hesitant to read it. I mean, suggested because Shawn’s only gonna go and buy it, but as called, “Writing a User Manual” by David Hewson I think. Anyway, so the Forward is by my favorite author Lee Child, and right at the beginning of the forward. So you can read the Forward for free by just doing the first chapter trick on Kindle. And so Lee Child writes, “My heart to book could be all of three words, ignore all advice.” And then he goes on to say that the only way to learn to write is to write. I think that that comes later.

[André] “Writing a User Manual.”
