[André] Yeah, I mean the short answer is, I’ve done this before, but I don’t know. And I had something coded by a coder in PHP, basically, any affiliate link. It doesn’t have to be specific to ClickBank. When it goes through the fill link, it drops a cookie and the person lands on your website or wherever the link redirects to. And it redirects that to a cookie and then all you do is on your opt-in form. It then looks for that cookie and then writes it to the custom field. And I’m saying this very generally now, because I don’t know how to do it. you’ll just need to get a coder, Brendan Dunn does this currently, and he uses ConvertKits, so I presume it’s, but then he’s a coder. So he’s probably just coded it up himself. But, I don’t think there’s an off-the-shelf solution other than just hiring a coder for five bucks an hour, off of fiber. So basically we’re answering questions about code as writers and about video as writers. So there you go. My dad doesn’t think about cooking. I got hungry though. Damn it! All right, here we go. Anonymous Q and A. Here we go, all right. Deep breaths, all right. “If I’m going to be selling through my story power promotion and product launch series, should I take it off my normal website? What’s the advantage disadvantage of doing that?” Wanna answer this one? We both cannot. I assume it means take the product off the website.

[André] I don’t know, it’s pretty cute.

[Shawn] So the way I’m reading this is if you sell through a story power promotion or PLS, you not just let somebody buy off the website. No, I mean, you could, but you don’t have to, I mean, they’re not in the don’t buy business, right? We had this conversation yesterday and I was really thought it where it ended was like really interesting, which is we’re in the happy customer business, right? So there are some people to be happy, they need to come buy your stuff right now. I know that because I’m one of them. I love you guys, but I’m not gonna read your seven part relationship building series. Most likely I’m gonna buy your stuff and then we’ll figure it out. I know the irony is not lost. But there are people like, I don’t know, there may be perfectly valid reasons to do this, right? So, hey, in our promotion, our PLS for Black Friday will be one of them, right? The Ideas to Assets Masterclass is not available for sale any other time of the year ever. We’ve only sold it once. We’re going to make, it’s something that we’re gonna make available for Black Friday, from Black Friday until Cyber Monday. It’s not on our website, right? So yes, there are times that, what’s the advantage and disadvantage of doing that? Well, if it’s a PLS, there’s an advantage that it’s time-bound, that’s what makes it a PLS. So should you take it off your website? No, you can leave the product on your website if you want, and have a waiting list, or take it off your site. And you have the list that you’re communicating with. If you take it off your site, there’s no reference to it. You only talk about it in email when people have raised their hand, it intensifies interest, it’s not available anytime. So of course, you’re gonna get a different type of response. And if somebody could, they don’t buy it today, they can just go next week. But let’s say for the sake of argument that the Ideas to Assets Masterclass was available year around. But for Black Friday, we discounted it. Well, okay, I’m leave it on the site year round, discount it for Black Friday. You don’t have to take it off the site. So, yeah, I mean, you could have, I mean, I think this may be true for backend offers more or more when you might have, it’s all structured business kind of questions. You might have something that everybody can buy, but that you don’t want everybody to buy unless they have moved closer to you in some capacity. They’ve purchased one thing before they purchase something else. Or they’ve watched a webinar, whatever it is. So there’re certain reasons to do it. There are certain reasons to do both, but there is no canonical capital T truth. You always do X or always do Y. Everything is context. Off to you.

– So one of the email recognizes the fact that people buy at different times. So this is the thing. Even if you have places on the website to buy the product, like we do, people can buy. And the majority of people don’t buy straight away, they’ll end up on the email list and they’ll buy later at some point later, between day one and day infinity. But I think there’s another way of reading that question. So let me unpack that. So some of the things that we do is, we’ll do a product launch series because we are interacting with our audience over that, over the duration of that product launch series. And many times or sometimes we don’t actually have in air quotes, sales page for the product, we made like on the art of email now. There’s no sales page. There’s a manifesto and there’s a button to buy, which is fine when we doing all the heavy lifting, through a PLS, which is what we did. We launched art of email internally to our audience that already knows us. So there was no need to have a proper sales page. So we did that through the PLS, but we also recognize the fact that some of the things we said in those emails, we cannot put on the website because it allows those people later on, where people that don’t know who we are, when they interacting with the website before they’ve come onto our email list, we will modify some of those PLS emails in a way like if you go to the durable business part of our website, you’ll see that we’ve got our previous PLS right there. I mean, it’s content pages and links on the product page for the durable business. So somebody that lands on that page called, they can see everything, there’s a buy button. They can reread that PLS in real time, on their own time. And it’ll either pull them to make a purchase or they’ll get directed to our front door. They’ll opt in, go through our emerging marketing series. Start to receive our newsletters and at some point make a purchase. So that PLS that we wrote, we’ve reused and repurposed and put that on the website. And we do this regularly. So art of email, some of the emails that we wrote will get repurposed and will end up on the art of email website, whenever we could run to doing it. And also it’ll inform what’ll be in a story power promotion for art of email at some point. It won’t be identical to what’s on the website ’cause that’ll be done. It’ll be some reflection of it, some version of it. So there’s nothing that we ever send out that is ever used once. It doesn’t exist. Everything is reused or repurposed in some way. We’ll externalize something through an email, that’ll inform other things. Everything is just used again. So I think that’s the other way of reading that question.

[Shawn] Let’s move on to Kim’s question. All right, so Kim asks, “I’d love to hear how you would think about a customer onboarding series in PLS when the purchase is a digital product. I saw digital and printable activities, child counselors use in their sessions.” So I’m assuming by digital product, you mean delivered digitally like PDF downloadable. That’s how I’m going to, is Kim with us here. Looking for Kim, there’s Kim. Kim, is that accurate when you say digital, like downloadable PDF? Okay, gotcha. I love when my thinking is accurate. So, you should answer this first so I can think about it.

[André] I’m trying to understand the question first.

[Shawn] All right, so I’ll answer it ’cause I think that I can explain it as I answer it. ‘Cause I’m thinking about a specific client that I worked with. So, how would you think about a customer onboarding series in PLS when you purchase as a digital product? So obviously lots of clients are digital delivery. So PDFs, MP3 files. Like just remember any particular clients did like MP3 files for meditation, audios, downloadable videos, back when that was a thing. So, the first thing of course that you deal with is delivery, right? So, somebody buys something and immediately after their purchase, the onboarding series is, that’s the first thing you need to do is get the thing in their hands. And generally that’s where everything stops, right? I mean, you get there’s no onboarding, my experience is very little onboarding series. So I guess the first question I would ask is, is this purchased, is this the beginning and the end, right? The alpha and the omega. You’ve sold them the thing and that’s it, there’s no more thing. Or is this purchase something that leads to other things that they can purchase? The reason I’m curious about that is, if somebody is buying something and it’s just digital fulfillment, then the focus of the customer onboarding series is just how to get the most value from what they purchased, right? So it’s email one, “Here’s how to download your stuff. Here’s what to do if we have any issues downloading things, let’s let us know and we’ll take care of it, this timeframe whatever.” Email two is, whatever the first thing you want to draw attention to, they’ve purchased, so you’re building a relationship with a paying customer. So you’re coming from this perspective of being thankful, sort of making it clear. “Hey, we really appreciate purchasing X, Y, Z. Over the next X number of days, we’re gonna draw attention to a few things to make sure that you get the most value from it. The feedback that we hear consistently is that, this is the most impactful part of whatever you just downloaded and you can find it on page 23. And here’s what so and so has, here three or four things to people have said about that and why it’s so valuable.” Like that’s kind of how you think about the customer onboarding series. It’s just how are you drawing attention to it? Now, if this is a purchase that leads to the possibility of by other stuff. So, maybe this is, I’m thinking about this particular client that I worked with, where you could buy, if you were interested in stress relief, you could get a meditation audio on stress relief, but they also had a series that, there was one on better sleep. And I think they had four or five things. And generally what we found are people who were interested in one or were interested in all of them. So if that’s the case, then the customer onboarding series has a couple of things that he needs to do. One, it needs to do the same thing a traditional one does, it just draw attention to the value that they just got. So if it’s a meditation audio, let people know, how often, like what are the common questions people have? Like, should I listen to this once a day, once a week, 10 times a day, like answer those questions. Like, again, you’re just trying to show people how to get the most value for them, from what they just purchased. These are reassuring of them that they just made a good decision, because in general, after people buy something, there’s a little bit of buyers remorse like, “Oh, is that stupid?” We all do that naturally. And we’re just offsetting buyers remorse, “Pat in the back, don’t worry, you made a great decision. We’re here for you, and this is why it was a great decision. Here’s how you’re gonna get value from it. Here’s how to like.” ‘Cause you know all this stuff and they don’t. And you’re a teacher in that sense. You’re just teaching them how to extract the most value from what they’ve purchased from you. If there’s more value in the future, like if there’s something else, don’t send them an email on day two that says, “Hey, I noticed you bought the sleep meditation audio. Did you know we have a stress relief one too?” I mean, come on, right? Too soon, right? Talk about two, three days, four days, like whatever. I mean, if it’s a $9 audio, you probably don’t need any three or four days of how to get the most out of it. But give them a few days, don’t buy into this idea though, they’re like in a buying heat and they just bought and get them to buy more. Don’t be that person. But tell ’em about why the thing they just purchased is a good idea, how they can get the most value from it. And once they’ve seen that it’s useful to them, then mention that there are other ways that they can get additional value. And if they’re interested in that, I would do a hard bridge at that point. I wouldn’t do a soft bridge. “If you’d like to know more about our entire catalog, you can click here to see other audio or other meditation audios or, we have a five part series where we explain how, like how to integrate the entire series, into your life in a way that’s better sleep, better, whatever, click here and we’ll send you that series.” Like that’s sort of an easy template to follow. So I’m gonna hand it back to you, André. ‘Cause there’s another part of the question, which is how do you do the PLS? And I don’t have a great answer for that yet. So I’m gonna let you, I’m gonna spend some time percolating while you’re spending some time talking.

[André] One of the best things with the customer onboarding series is that, someone’s just made a purchase and you have the attention and it’s an opportunity to delight them in ways that is more than what they’ve just received. So one way to think about this is, is the customer onboarding series could be missing chapters. So it’s more not the same, but so it’s not, “In chapter three we discuss blah, blah, blah. Make sure that you go and read chapter three.” Not that, it’s different ideas or angles or ways of looking at whatever’s in there, but through this lens of a missing chapter. So it becomes valuable all by itself almost to the point that if they receive an onboarding series or by itself, without the actual PDF download, it would be as valuable if not more valuable than what they’ve just purchased. So when you join those two things together, the two plus two, the experience that they’re feeling is gonna be more than four. It’s gonna be compounded. So I think that’s enough frame to use with a customer onboarding series. It’s an opportunity to really delight somebody in a way that they won’t expect or they weren’t expecting. And then you can hand off to, as Shawn mentioned, and you could hand off to showcase series. “So, we have a showcase series on ‘insert topic.’ If you ever sit in this, click here and we’ll send it to you in a showcase series.” Is really just showcasing something. It’s unpacking it to the point where it’s gonna be an absolute natural purchase for somebody without you trying to sell on it. It’s just you unpacking art of emails, sphere of influence or something, to a point that at the end of it, somebody knows precisely if this is something for them right now or maybe later, but they’ve raised their hand to get that thing. So it’s not a story part promotion. I wouldn’t necessarily attach story apart promotions to a customer onboarding series. Although, you can do anything you want. I would probably do showcase series and attach those, which is just an expression of a soap opera series.

[Shawn] All right, so the other part of this question is hard, right? Jack would be a good person to answer this one, Jack Boyd. So if I’m understanding the question well, which is, “How do you do a PLS when the purchase is a digital product.” Which I assume is not like, there’s no open and close, right? You’re not saying, “We’re taking us off the market forever.” None of that bullshit. So I mean, there’s an obvious answer. And then not a so obvious answer. The obvious answer is, if it’s not time-bound, then it’s not a PLS, it’s a story power promotion. So that may be the answer, you know where that leads. If you want to do it as a PLS, which you could, right? You can do a, I mean, you can do a PLS for anything if there’s a reason to make it time-bound, a legitimate reason for the record, to make it time-bound. And a legitimate reason might be something like Black Friday is certainly a time when everybody discounts and there’s some expectation. So if there’s a, I think I saw that you’re serving the teacher market at some point. So if you’re serving the teacher market, You could do a, I don’t know when teachers buy their stuff. If it’s at the end of summer or beginning of like, if the budget of the other part of the So I just, I guess that part is irrelevant, but you can do time-bound promotions with discounts, if there’s a reason to do them. And yes, you can make up reasons to do them. I’m on an email list where I swear to God, every three days is the sum insert, whatever thing you sell. It’s the most ridiculous and mind numbingly annoying thing, but it’s just giving people reasons to buy. I mean, I think in general, you have a business that benefits from a story powered promotion, do that. And occasionally if you want to, if there’s a good reason to do something that is time-bound, and it can be something, I mean, it doesn’t have to be X percent off. It can be so thing like, I mean, you may know for example that, in August, sales slump, right? That’s kind of a truism. And you may do something where you say, “Whatever.” In the last week of August, you have this thing where you can normally all, your courses or the thing your digital products are $10 each individually, or you can buy all 10 for $90. But because during the last week of August, once a year, you do the XYZ Kim’s board sale. And you can buy all your stuff for $70. And it happens once a year, it’s PLS. That’s fine. Like, there’s nothing wrong with that. Just I think when you make a habit of doing a discount PLS then people just expect that you’re going to discount. So you just can’t use that all the time. What do you do after you use it all the time that’s how I’ve thought about it. Yeah, it’s something Kim mentioned in the chat here the time-bound discount at the launch of a new product. We do that, all right? So, it’s cannon ball’s idea when we’re creating something new. We let people know, this doesn’t exist yet. And in exchange, there’s cohort one, we do this all the time. Cohort one gets a discount because this thing doesn’t exist yet. And we’re kind of, changing the tires on the bus as we’re driving it down the road and because you’re taking that risk with us, it’s cost less. It’s gonna be more in the future, but we don’t discount it in the future. We’re true to our word. So, yes, they’re always, as long as the reason why feels right to you, then I think it’s okay. André your thoughts on this one?

[André] Yeah, I mean, I’ve done discounts a lot in the past and it got to a point where I made a decision. This was, before Shawn joined that any release product is never discounted ever again. ‘Cause I was having people that, and there’s nothing wrong with it because I’m the one that actually did it, right? So on my birthday once a year, I would discount some products like a certain product or all of them, and people could get out to 50% off. Well, people then missed that or knew that I was gonna do it. They would wait an entire year and not purchase and wait for the discount period and then buy one or multiple products on that discount period, which is fine because that’s how I did it until I realized the stupidity. Well, I mean, stupidity, it’s just that seemed weird. I mean, the people were just doing what I was giving them an opportunity to do so at that point, it was, “Okay, we are not doing discounts anymore ever for a release product.” We do them when we, as Shawn said, when we release, when we creating something and we are interacting with somebody, so it’s not a finished thing yet, that’s different. And so it makes our Black Fridays far more challenging because it’s not as easy as just saying, “Hey, here’s a 50% discount on insert product.” We’ve we create something from scratch or do something different. It just makes it harder, but it’s more of a surprise we think. So I think you just need to make a decision that will have your eyes wide open, that if you do offer discounts, people will expect discounts, and some will even wait for those discounts and will not purchase until there’s a discount window and that will be the opportunity to make these purchases. But if there’s no discounts, people are still gonna buy. They’re just not gonna wait for the discount windows all the time. So just be mindful of that.

[Shawn] All right, let’s move on to the next question. I love this next question. Anonymous, even better. “I know that this has nothing do with the art of email, but why do you all not use a multi-age pre-sale site for your subscribe page when you teach multi-age pre-sale sites? Damn you two, you’re not congruent.” Oh, they didn’t put that in there, I made that up. “Your subscribe page is a single page. What’s the thinking here? Is it a single page pre-sale site okay?” I’m sorry, I read that as in like my mean tweet voice, and that may not be how it was intended at all. Well, first of all, you’re wrong. So you’re making an assumption. Let’s externalize the assumption that you’re making. You’re making the assumption that we drive traffic to our subscribe page and we don’t, we drive traffic to our homepage. And the expectation is you’re going to read, we are creators first. And then you’re going, ’cause you’ll see at the end, there’s no subscribe at the bottom of that, right? We expect people to show up in our world, we send traffic to the homepage, and then subscribe after that. So subscribe is actually, and from that view, is the second part. “Now, is it deliberately written as a two part multi-age pre-sale we have to click through?” No, you can’t click through, so maybe I’m wrong and you’re right. Not that it really matters. “So why don’t we have a multi-pay.” That’s a good question. Why don’t we? No, it just didn’t feel right. It feels right as one page. And it works, right? Our opt-in rate kind of where we like it. So, and that’s not trying to sound glib, but there’s nothing, there are millions of reasons in sphere of influence goes to a bunch of them to use a multi-age pre-sale site. It just doesn’t happen to fit with this particular page on our site. Now, we do have a plan, a top secret plan, that we can’t tell you all of the details. But we will have a multi page entry point, into TLB at some point. But not yet. Now the other part of the question is, “What’s your thinking here?” Sorry, I like to use that voice, “Is a single page pre-sale site, okay?” Well, let’s ask our buddy Jonathan Boyd, Jonathan Boyd, who we’ve interviewed, and you could find that interview on the website, built a business literally from zero, to multiple millions of dollars in like six months. Now, I don’t know if he doubled again in the next six months. And Jonathan’s business, as he described to us, is based entirely on the time Google display network traffic to a 1600 page opt-in, single page based on the principles he learned in LBC and sphere of influence. So that draws attention and pulls attention down on the page. At the time he had its only a 7% opt-in rate, but when you opt in, there’s a $27 offer, that he was monetizing the cost to acquire his traffic, his AOV, average order of value. And that first sale exceeded his cost to acquire a customer, with a multimillion dollar business scaled from zero in less than six months with a single 1600 page pre-sale. So if the question is, “Is a single page pre-sale site okay?” The answer is, fuck yeah, it is okay! There’s my F bomb for the day. I try not to say that, but there its. And this point, there it is. Not 1600 pages, David, sorry, 1600 words. Did I say 1600 pages? You know what meant? Yeah, and I mean, that’s the new course we’re coming out with. And it’s based on this model of, because the only way we give refunds is if you show us your 1600 page pre-sell that’s not working and then we’ll give you a refund. ‘Cause no one’s gonna write 1600 pages. That’s a joke for all of you who are like, “Really!” No, we wouldn’t do that. Okay, what is your thought on this one? You’re the one who designed this. It’s not my fault. You did it.

[André] Well, multi pages is just one expression of what’s codified within sphere of influence. For example, you can have a single page that does the same thing essentially, minus the click through to the next pages. So we’ve got many multi-age things inside of the TLB world. If you click around, you can find them. Most of them are framed as manifestos. Some of them are three pages. Some of them are 10 pages deep, but this is inside of the world ’cause we do it like that’s because we want people to find them. So our homepage is the start point. And we are not presuming, I mean, in our expression of it. we’re not presuming that we are gonna create a linear thing that is gonna fit with everybody. So if somebody goes to our homepage, which just it’s an expression of how we show up, what matters for us. And then we leave it for them to do a choose your own adventure, which is again, this is another expression of a multipage. Some people might just click on the subscribe link or it’s also linked to from on the homepage contextually, and they’ll end up over there, they’ll read that, and that will become their two step multi page. Others will click through to our learn tab, which is our blog and they’ll go deeper. They might end up on a proper multi page, which is one of our manifestos. We’ve got many on there. And then they’ll click through next, next, next. And that’ll be this journey, this rabbit hole that they’ve just been swallowed up by. So there’s no one way to do this. You can do it as single pages. You can do it as a two-gen adventure style and allow people to find those pods themselves, which is super powerful. We could send many emails from people saying that they’ve read this page and that page and just got sucked in and it’s never the same. No one person has ever sent the same email where they’ve gone down the same pages because it’s a nonlinear world we’ve created. And when you’re starting out and you just wanna create a single path for somebody, sure, you can do a multi page that then ends in a opportunity for or somebody to get on your email list. That’s the easiest first step. It can be a single page like Jonathan Boyd, that have to read 1600 words that’s been structured in such a way that by the time somebody gets to the opt-in, they’ve had this insight and they want to play the guitar in this way that’s been expressed by Jonathan. So they opt in. And 70% do and then they’ll buy. And then they get introduced into Jonathan’s world that way. There is no one way that’s one size it fits all here. And I think over time, every business should turn into a world building exercise, just like we mentioned about, maybe in the last call or the call before that. But her world is nonlinear. Even though if you look at her home page, it can feel linear. It’s a sales page, there’s a video sales data, there’s a button to buy. It seems very linear. But when you really look at the site, it is not linear. She doesn’t hide all the buttons away. it’s an expensive world that people can wander down in a way that matters to them. And there’ll be insights along the way. There’ll be a moment where someone says “I’m gonna become a customer before they click on a button and become a customer.” It’s sometimes long before. “Heres is a $297 a month investment.” So that moment that I’m gonna become a customer probably happens quite soon. I mean, quite, further in front of actually clicking on the button and making the purchase. So yeah, that’s my slot ramble.

[Shawn] Yeah, I was gonna add something and I don’t think it’s, I don’t even know, move on to the next one. Yeah, all right, let’s move on to the next one. Let’s see. So this is from Carma. Carma asks, “Is making your VNL, your value newsletter automated a bad thing necessarily?” No, I mean, so we put in the name what the value newsletter is all about, first word value, right? We could, let’s just say for the sake of argument that we wanted to take three months off next year, we’re just gonna take January through March and do whatever. I don’t know, we’re sitting on beach somewhere, which is ridiculous ’cause us on a beach is like a fork in the microwave, but let’s just assume we’re gonna do that. We could write, whatever that is, eight, six, sorry, six, seven different newsletters in advance, cue ’em all up, and send them. I mean, as long as they’re giving value, now we might miss a little of whatever, but we don’t follow along in our market, we’re not following along with the zeitgeist. Now, if apple drops some bomb or Facebook drops some bomb, and we didn’t like ever mention that, maybe some people would notice, but we’re not in a timely business. We’re in a value business. So, of course you can. I mean, it’s all contextual, and I mean, you could queue up a year. So I don’t know. I mean, I don’t think I’d go too far in advance, but you could. I mean, if you have a greatest hits thing, and Carma, I know the situation that you’re dealing with, because you shared it with me and I understand why you would want to be, why sort of the question behind this. So let’s assume that, without giving anything away and protecting Carma’s privacy, let’s assume I’ll come up with something that’s same idea that doesn’t share any details, which is, let’s assume that for some reason I need to not work next year, whatever reason. But I also need to make some income. So let’s say for the sake of our argument that I need to create a business that does that. And the question being asked is, how could I do that? Where the newsletter is still a component of that system, but I’m not going to be able to create them next year, for whatever reason, I’m taking the year off and traveling. No internet access, but I need the benefit of the email. I just have to write ’em in advance and load ’em up. And there’s no reason I couldn’t do everything that we teach and the art of email. I could have some multipart themed series. I could have some one offs. I could have some content on a website. I could have some content in an email, I could have in depth. I could have best of, I mean, you name it. I could do all of that. I would just have to do the work in advance and load ’em all into the system, and schedule the system to send them, that’s it. So yeah, you absolutely can do what you’re describing. André, I’m sure have some thoughts on this? Yeah, I mean, let me cover a few scenarios then. I think having a best hits, so between a relationship building series and adding somebody to your VNL, to your live VNL loop, you could have a best hits which is, but, and the nice thing with the best hits is, you get to choose the newsletters that will take somebody down a path that exposes them to parts of your business in a way that’s predictable, ’cause you are in control over it. You’ve you know which emails they’re gonna get. And let’s say the best hits is one month long. It’s a newsletter once a week. So four newsletters, maybe there’s eight. If you wanna do twice a week or over two months. So then then they get that. So now that person has a very deep relationship pretty quickly now because they’ve received these newsletters in an order that you want them to receive them in. At which point it can then move to your live loop. So when you’re sending out the lives ones. Only people that have gone through the automated one, then gets the live ones. That’s one expression. I think that’s a nice way of doing it. I probably wouldn’t want people to be in an automated one for too long because then they missing all the live ones where there’s, which is mechanisms to introduce people to PLSs and stuff like that. You can schedule broadcasts, which is the scenario that Shawn first mentioned, but then you can also create a sequence. So if you wanted to do this for an entire year, you could create a evergreen newsletter sequence and stack your one year’s worth of newsletter inside of this. So you know people are gonna get it sequentially. But then you can use the automation that we share in module 11, that within the context of those newsletters that are asking people to raise hands, so it’s got the hard bridges built into those newsletters that you know are going out at a predictable frequency. And it’s exposing people to story power promotions. If somebody clicks to raise their hand for a story power promotion, for example, because you obviously in that scenario, you’re not gonna be able to do PLSs. So, it’ll be story power promotion, somebody raises their hand. The automation would unsubscribe them from the newsletter sequence. So it’ll unsubscribe them from the evergreen newsletter sequence that would go through the automated promotion, they’ll either buy or they won’t buy. And then it’ll subscribe them back to the same sequence. And the nice thing with the convert kit. Not sure about other ESPs, is it will insert them where they left off. So you can tell the ConvertKits that a certain sequence, people can only subscribe to it once. So once they get to the end of it, it’s done. They can never resubscribe to it again. And that’s a setting you you can choose when you’re choosing your, when you’re creating your sequence. So if you set it that people can only subscribe to it once, then that’s perfect because when you resubscribe them to it, using the automation, once they’ve finished a story power promotion, it’ll just put them back where they’re left off. So they’ll just start to get the next newsletter, as if nothing ever happened, which is perfect. So you could have an evergreen business that runs for a year plus because whenever they go into these promotional sequences, it kind of pauses them in the newsletter side. Yeah, and you can run the business and make money, while you’re sleeping or doing whatever you’re doing for a year. That’s another way to do it.

[Shawn] Bittersweet, we’re down to the last question, which I’m pretty sure André, actually submitted this question. I don’t have any evidence of this, but this one’s teed up a little bit too nicely. And–

[André] I think it’s the last one.

[Shawn] And it’s the last one, it’s a little shout out to himself. There’s a lot going on in this question that makes me think André actually wrote this question. He’s fine, right?” Okay, so this is a funny setup because, so two years ago before we were business partners, the first project we did together, we were working, we were consulting together for a very large financial publisher and André flew to, he flew to New York city. We hold up in a two bed room suite outside of New York city for the week of Thanksgiving. And we went from having literally nothing to other than some ideas, and to the end of that experience, which was Thanksgiving, we needed to have a finished asset that we could give to this client. It was a, well, I believe it’s three, maybe four or page front end for this client. So what we did, and you can see I think all of the promotion is available online. I think it’s in the art of email too. We link to all of it, but you can see, the ideation behind it and which became, we call it Ideas to Assets, but it was an expression at the time of that experience. So we recorded, and it was fun because that was the first time we’d ever written together. It was the first time either of us had written with another person, which was really weird, and sort of how that evolved and how we created something. And what it’s like to go from an idea, or a bunch of ideas, to actually a finished product. And this client is, they’re in the top 00001% in what they do. So this is not, something we could just throw together. It had to be really, really good. So we captured that, we called it Ideas to Assets Masterclass. Two years ago, we sold it on Black Friday, and to culmination of that experience. So for this year for Black Friday, and really as a I think if we’re being honest, is a treat to ourselves, we’ve done a lot of work this year. So this Black Friday rolls around, we wanted to do something, make something available to our audience, but we also, I think one of the factors was, we wanted to do something that was just for fun, right? So, but we could just re-released Ideas to Assets, but that seemed kind of, I mean, that was a slice and time. It was two years ago, we weren’t collaborating. So what we decided to do is, we are updating Ideas to Assets, so that it’s going to be, it’s really gonna look at the evolution, right? So you’ll see the first thing all the stuff that was in the first thing, which is just a lot of fun. But then it’s going to be, we’re going to add on our individual PKM approaches, that would be André and mine for André who wrote the question. And we’ll because our systems are very different. They overlap in some ways, but they’re very, very different than others. And then we’re also going to, I think and most of this will be video or a screen-cast. And then we’re also going to explain how we collaborate because we’re both individual thinkers, but everything that you read to be clear, every single thing that you read, we both write. So, and I mention that because that that’s a very different type of creative work. When two people are making something, is very different than one person making something. So Ideas to Assets will be available from Black Friday through Cyber Monday. We don’t know pricing yet. We gotta figure that out. It’s not a full course. We don’t sell it any time, the rest of the year. but that’s really, that’s to date the closest thing that we have that’s really, and it’s really shows how we do, really it’s in the name, how we go from the, and there’s also a two part, there’s like the next two value newsletters will shine some light at the 10,000 foot view of our creative process and how it maps to the creative process outlined by, Richard Benson and “The Breakout Principle,” which is, I won’t tell you off the, our parts, ’cause we only tell you two parts in the first email, kind of break the open loop. But the first part is struggle, right? That’s the work, it’s taking all the stuff in. And that’s the first part of personal knowledge management and interestingly that’s where most people stop, they just collect, right? Just, I read this book and I there’s this quote and there’s this thing and they collect, collect, collect, collect, collect. Well, that’s part of it. That’s a small part of the system. And what we really want to draw attention to is, yes, there’s collection, but that collection, those inputs, the stuff we read, the stuff, we watch, the stuff we listen to, percolates into ideas that then emerge. And then those ideas that are emergent properties of the inputs, also get jammed together and smashed into other things and bounced around with each other and with colleagues, and then something even more emerges from those. So we want to draw some attention to that. So André, “Anonymous,” teed that up really well with this question. Which to be clear was not intentional, but that is coming. If that’s something, if you already own Ideas to Assets, you get the upgrade for free of course, that’s just true for everything. If you don’t own it, you’ll just see what it looks like over the next, the two newsletters will tee it up. We’re gonna do a PLS over the course of Thanksgiving week Thanksgiving here in US, curtain will open Friday curtain will close Monday. Anything you wanna contribute to that?

[André] Unless he’s using the course to, he’s either adapting it and turning it into something that’s not the public domain version, based on the lessons in it. So maybe he’s got some extra comment–

[Shawn] I think you’re right. I think you should answer this question.

[André] Well, I think–

[Shawn] You want fiction, right? Okay, so you’re exactly right. So you should answer the question ’cause you clearly understand it better than I do.

[André] I think broadly, there would be some multi page before the opt-in, or a single long page. And it would create enough tension that the person or the people that would be interested in said thing would then opt-in, at which point I think the RBS could then introduce them to the idea that you’ve done. And then at the end of the RBS, it could transition into a full soap opera series that unpacks this thing. I suppose you could do it all in a single RBS. I mean, there’s no reason why you couldn’t. I just functionally inside of the email system, I’d probably keep an RBS and soap opera series separately as separate. So then it makes it easier to modify, to change. So the RBS could be changed independently of the soap opera series. So I would have them separate, even though they experience that somebody is going through may be consistent, may be the same, like a one long linear thing. I would keep them separate inside of the ESP to make it easier for you to edit and change and do whatnot. And then obviously a hard bridge to the course, yeah.

[Shawn] Yeah, I don’t really have anything to add to this other than what I call the house of pizza metaphor. So in order to know what the metaphor is, you have to understand the backstory. I grew up in a very small town, from junior high on population about 1500. Somebody opened a pizza place. I believe it was when I was in ninth grade, somewhere when I was entering my freshman year of high school, that pizza place changed hands at least 10 times over the years before the building eventually burned down. That was the only thing that got people to stop buying the pizza place and trying to make it work. It would not have taken anybody who bought that place more than about 10 minutes with a pencil and a piece of paper and you don’t even need a calculator, but you could figure out in about 10 minutes that the limits of what that business was likely to produce and the demands required to produce that, it would be very easy to see that that business was never going to get somebody to a point where it made sense financially. So I don’t know if this’ the business that you’re describing will or will not make sense financially, but when you are selling something that people can get for free, you are automatically starting with, your starting, let’s just say with a disadvantage. So it’s not a perfect fit to the house of pizza metaphor, but I would encourage you before you put all of the work into the creation required to make this happen, to sell something that somebody can buy, somebody we can just go get for free. Maybe consider an alternative where the thing that you sell eventually isn’t available for free. Now that doesn’t mean, it could be very well be that you just change how you’re thinking about it. So you can say, so the fictional story is based on a public domain course, but you mentioned later that you’re thinking about selling that course. Don’t sell that course, sell your course inspired by what you’ve learned in that course, or reference it if it has some sort of like, I mean, I remember years ago, someone did a really good a course on Napoleon Hill’s book, “Think and Grow Rich.” And it was very well done. That book is in the public domain as well, but you didn’t need the book because the point of the course was that, the book has so much depth and there’s so much nuance and this is part of the mythos of, “Think and Grow Rich” is that you’re going to have an aha moment at some time and you have to have that aha moment to really get it. And this course was a like how to maximize your understanding of the book so that you could have that experience. So the book just became a vehicle and the vehicle was the thing, that’s what drove the energy. But anybody could get that book in the public domain very easily. So it was never about the value that was being offered, it was never about the book. The value proposition was that this book is this treasure. But if you just read the book, you’re not going to get the real value from it. And the course is the way to extract all the value out of it. So I would suggest to you so that you don’t paint yourself into a corner when in inevitably someone’s either going to explicitly say to you or implicitly make the choice on their own. “Why would I pay you for something that I can go get for free?” The answer is, “You can’t get. You can’t get what I’m offering for free, cause I’m not offering the course, I’m offering this thing that extracts the value that you can’t get from just going through the course. And here’s how and here’s why I know that.” Like, it’s all in how you subtly place those two things. I would add that one thought to the question. We did it, we did it in two and a half hours too, wow! Who’d have guessed.

[André] That’s a record. Another record.

– So this is bittersweet, right? This is the last Q and A call for the live workshop. We do next week, we will record workshop content, and we will record a review. So please submit. If you want something reviewed, please submit it to us by next week. I think I’ll close with two things and then handoff to André. So the first thing is, I can’t say thank you enough. You’ve given us the most precious thing that you have, which is your attention, and that is never lost on us. It is always a privilege and always an honor, and we don’t use those words lightly to have your attention and have these opportunities with you and so thank you. And then the second part is, the workshop’s like everything that we do are dynamic. This first time through, was really an opportunity just to understand it was interact with all of you in a more direct way and to understand really how, first of all, if the workshop could be helpful and then how it could be helpful. It will get repackaged in some ways, maybe restructured in some ways, I have no doubt, just because of how we do things that we will add to it. There will be things that we want to include in here. And when we do that, we’ll just let you know. You’ll get emails. You own this, you own the art of email and you own the workshop for life. We will make them better. That’s just how we approach things. And you’re inside the tent now and you will always get those updates until, both of us take our last breath. So thank you. Thank you for your trust. And thank you for your attention. André, take us home.

[André] Yeah, of course. How do I better that? Yeah, just wanna reiterate what Shawn just said. And this has been a real honor for both of us. This is why we do this stuff, this is what gets us outta bed in the mornings. We think 2022 is gonna be really exciting, for reasons that we are still trying to figure out ourselves. But we have a sense that it’s gonna be an interesting journey for everyone, you guys included, So that’s all I can say. But thanks for your attention. This has been awesome.

[Shawn] And I guess the last point to make before we close is that, all praise and accolades, make sure you put my name in the subject line, those come to me, any complaints, make sure those go to André. All right, thanks everybody.

[André] Take care everyone.
